agent_raw:
The retrospective agreement study does not justify this deployment. The most fundamental problem is that historical authorization outcomes are not a stable measure of appropriate oncology care — they reflect payer policy as it existed at different points in a study period when guidelines changed repeatedly, biosimilars entered use, and billing pathways shifted. A model trained to match those historical decisions will produce outputs that lag behind evolving medical standards. This is especially problematic for a deployment that would automate denials, where a policy-lagged model could systematically block access to regimens that are newly guideline-concordant.

Second, urgent cases handled outside the standard queue through expedited physician-to-physician review are underrepresented or poorly labeled in the training data. These are precisely the cases where an incorrect fast denial — or a failure to flag a case as urgent — would be most harmful.

Third, performance is reported only in aggregate, concealing variation by cancer type, therapy line, and policy vintage. A model that performs well on common, well-established chemotherapy regimens may fail on immunotherapy combinations, later-line treatments, or recently approved biosimilar substitutions.

Fourth, the deployment conflates automated approvals with faster denials. In oncology, a wrongly denied authorization can delay treatment. This is not symmetric with a wrongly approved one in terms of patient risk, but agreement metrics treat all disagreements equally.

The appropriate path is to restrict any initial deployment to auto-approvals for a pre-specified narrow set of stable, guideline-concordant regimens with ongoing physician audit, and to require subgroup and policy-vintage analysis before any scope expansion.
all_issues_raised: Historical authorization outcomes encode shifting payer policy
Urgent cases underrepresented in structured queue
Aggregate metrics hide subgroup performance variation
